summary: The 32nd U.S. president (1933-1945), who led the country through the Great Depression with his New Deal relief and reform programs and through most of World War II. Clues most often identify him through his New Deal advisors (the "Brain Trust," Frances Perkins, Harry Hopkins), his unprecedented run for a third and fourth term, his 1937 court-packing plan, his Good Neighbor policy toward Latin America, and his radio broadcasts known as the fireside chats. He served as Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Woodrow Wilson, ran for vice president in 1920, and led the country from Pearl Harbor until his death in office in April 1945.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The 32nd president of the United States (1933-1945), most often identified in giveaways through his New Deal economic reforms, his library at Hyde Park, New York, and his unprecedented election to four terms in office. He is commemorated by numerous namesake sites, including a memorial in Washington, D.C.'s Tidal Basin featuring George Segal sculptures of Depression-era Americans, and Franklin Delano Roosevelt Island in the East River (formerly Welfare Island).

examples: name this president whose library is in Hyde Park, New York.

examples: A memorial to this man in DC's Tidal Basin features George Segal sculptures of people listening to a radio broadcast and waiting in a breadline.

clues.clue: An island in the East River, formerly called Welfare Island, is named for him
examples: An island named for this man in the East River used to be called Welfare Island.

clues.clue: Lake Roosevelt, the primary reservoir for Stevens County, Washington, was formed by the Grand Coulee Dam and named for him
examples: A lake named for this man is the primary reservoir for Stevens County, Washington and was formed by the Grand Coulee Dam.

clues.clue: Elected to an unprecedented four terms as president
examples: what president outlined his court-packing plan in fireside chats?
identify this decision that allowed an incumbent president to defeat Wendell Willkie in the 1940 election.

works.name: Early Career and Path to the Presidency
works.description: Roosevelt served as Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Secretary Josephus Daniels during the Wilson administration, in which role he helped establish the North Sea Mine Barrage to stop the movement of German U-boats around Britain. He ran unsuccessfully for vice president in 1920 as James Cox's running mate before serving as governor of New York, and after becoming president he rewarded his old boss Daniels by appointing him U.S. Ambassador to Mexico.

clues.clue: As Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Josephus Daniels, he helped establish the North Sea Mine Barrage to stop German U-boats
examples: To stop the movement of U-Boats around Britain, this Assistant Secretary of the Navy helped establish the North Sea Mine Barrage.

clues.clue: Ran unsuccessfully for vice president in 1920 as James Cox's running mate, and later served as governor of New York
examples: Later, as Woodrow Wilson's Secretary of the Navy, Daniels would be in charge of managing this Assistant Secretary of the Navy, who would later serve as James Cox's running mate and governor of New York before becoming president.

clues.clue: As president, he rewarded his old boss Josephus Daniels by appointing him U.S. Ambassador to Mexico
examples: After becoming president, Roosevelt rewarded his old boss by appointing him to this position.

works.name: The New Deal: Relief, Recovery, and Reform
works.description: Roosevelt's response to the Great Depression relied on an original "Brain Trust" of Columbia professors including Rexford Tugwell and Adolf Berle, and later on close advisors like Harry Hopkins, who ran the Works Progress Administration and later became FDR's key emissary to Britain, and Frances Perkins, the first female Cabinet member as Secretary of Labor, who helped draft the 1935 Social Security Act (upheld against a benefits-eligibility challenge in the case Flemming v. Nestor). The administration also passed the Agricultural Adjustment Act and expanded the National Park Service through the Civilian Conservation Corps under Robert Fechner. The National Industrial Recovery Act's authorization of "straight killing" codes for meat producers was struck down by the Supreme Court in the "sick chicken" case (Schechter Poultry v. United States).

clues.clue: Was advised by an original "Brain Trust" of Columbia professors including Rexford Tugwell and Adolf Berle, author of The Modern Corporation and Private Property
examples: Name this New Deal President who was advised by Tugwell and other members of the "Brain Trust."
This fellow Columbia professor and writer of The Modern Corporation and Private Property also served as a member of Roosevelt's original "Brain Trust" along with Rexford Tugwell.

clues.clue: Advisor Harry Hopkins ran the Works Progress Administration and later served as his primary emissary to Winston Churchill, overseeing Lend-Lease
examples: This FDR advisor ran the Works Progress Administration. Before American entrance into World War II, he coordinated unofficial negotiations with Winston Churchill.
This supervisor of the WPA was one of Roosevelt's closest advisors...He served as FDR's primary emissary to Britain during WWII and oversaw the lend lease program.

clues.clue: Frances Perkins, the first woman appointed to a presidential Cabinet, served as his Secretary of Labor and helped draft the 1935 Social Security Act
examples: This woman helped draft the Social Security Act as Roosevelt's Secretary of Labor.
Perkins served as Secretary of Labor under this president.

clues.clue: Signed the Social Security Act in 1935, creating a national old-age pension system later upheld against a benefits-eligibility challenge in Flemming v. Nestor
examples: The Social Security Act, which created a national old-age pension system, was passed by Congress in 1934 during the presidency of this man.
This Supreme Court case established that there is no contractual right to Social Security benefits.

clues.clue: Signed the Agricultural Adjustment Act, under which Henry A. Wallace served as his second vice president

examples: A president whose mother belonged to this family expanded the National Park Service through a conservation program headed by Robert Fechner.

examples: Coughlin initially supported but then opposed this president, remarking that America could choose him "or Ruin." Coughlin cast this president's landmark series of work programs as "Christ's Deal."

works.name: Court-Packing Plan and the Supreme Court
works.description: After the Supreme Court struck down several New Deal measures on "Black Monday," Roosevelt proposed a plan to add up to six new justices, one for every sitting justice over 70 -- a plan that ultimately failed in Congress, in part after ally Joseph Taylor Robinson died of a heart attack during the fight, alienating supporter Frank Gannett. The Court's own turn toward upholding New Deal legislation on "White Monday" was signaled by Justice Owen Roberts's so-called "switch in time that saved nine." Roosevelt's authority to remove a Federal Trade Commission member was denied in Humphrey's Executor v. United States, though he successfully nominated Robert Jackson to both his Cabinet and later the Supreme Court.

clues.clue: His plan to add up to six new justices failed in Congress in part after ally Joseph Taylor Robinson died of a heart attack, turning ally Frank Gannett against it
examples: Frank Gannett was a news media ally who turned against a plan by this president that failed partly due to Joseph Taylor Robinson's death from a heart attack.

clues.clue: "Black Monday" was a defeat and "White Monday" a victory for his agenda at the Supreme Court
examples: "Black Monday" and "White Monday" were respectively defeats and victories for this president, who nominated Robert Jackson to both the Cabinet and the Supreme Court.

clues.clue: Nominated Robert Jackson to both his Cabinet and later the Supreme Court

examples: A Court shift towards upholding this President's policies was signaled by Owen Roberts's "switch in time that saved nine."

examples: This president's authority to fire a member of the FTC was denied in a case argued by the executors of the estate of the man in question, William Humphrey.

works.name: The Election of 1940 and the Third Term
works.description: Roosevelt's break with the two-term tradition set by George Washington drew intense speculation, with cartoonists depicting him as the inscrutable Sphinx before he accepted a carefully stage-managed nomination at the 1940 Democratic National Convention (a crowd chanting for him was reportedly egged on by the "voice from the sewers," Ed Kelly loyalist Thomas Garry). His decision to run derailed the political ambitions of rivals James Farley and John Nance Garner and, after he defeated Republican Wendell Willkie, later prompted passage of the 22nd Amendment limiting future presidents to two terms. A supporter delighted crowds by rhyming the names of his 1940 congressional opponents, "Martin, Barton, and Fish."

clues.clue: Speculation over whether he would break with tradition and seek a third term led cartoonists to depict him as the Sphinx
examples: Increasing public speculation about this decision led political cartoonists to depict its central figure as the Sphinx.

clues.clue: His 1940 nomination was followed by a supporter's crowd-pleasing rhyme of the names "Martin, Barton, and Fish"
examples: In the aftermath of this decision, a politician delighted crowds by repeatedly rhyming the names "Martin, Barton, and Fish."

clues.clue: His decision to seek a third term sank the political ambitions of James Farley and John Nance Garner and later prompted passage of the 22nd Amendment
examples: The fallout from this decision, which sank the political ambitions of James Farley and John Nance Garner, caused the passage of the 22nd Amendment.

clues.clue: Defeated Republican Wendell Willkie in the 1940 election to win a third term
examples: identify this decision that allowed an incumbent president to defeat Wendell Willkie in the 1940 election.

clues.clue: Predicted to defeat Alf Landon in the 1936 election by George Gallup's newly-founded polling company
examples: One prominent Nebraska Admiral is George Gallup, who founded a polling company that made its name after predicting that this man would defeat Alf Landon in the 1936 presidential election.

works.name: Good Neighbor Policy and Latin America
works.description: Roosevelt's Good Neighbor policy sought closer ties with Latin America: at the 1933 Montevideo Convention he helped codify the declarative theory of statehood (with Sophonisba Breckinridge attending as a female delegate) and, through Secretary of State Cordell Hull, sent Walt Disney on a tour of South America that produced the film Saludos Amigos, in which the singer Carmen Miranda's work was later criticized as exoticizing. To remedy wartime labor shortages he began the Bracero Program admitting Mexican farmworkers (ended by his successor), and his administration drafted Haiti's constitution and ended the U.S. occupation of the country.

clues.clue: Helped codify the declarative theory of statehood at the 1933 Montevideo Convention, attended by female delegate Sophonisba Breckinridge
examples: This leader codified the declarative theory of statehood at an international conference attended by female representative Sophonisba Breckinridge.

clues.clue: Announced the Good Neighbor policy at the Montevideo Convention, under which he commissioned the film Saludos Amigos
examples: At the Montevideo Convention, this leader announced an initiative under which he commissioned the film Saludos Amigos.

clues.clue: Singer Carmen Miranda was criticized for her work made under his administration's Latin American outreach
examples: Samba performer Carmen Miranda was criticized for her work under this leader, who drafted the constitution of Haiti and later ended its occupation.

clues.clue: His administration drafted Haiti's constitution and ended the U.S. occupation of the country
examples: who drafted the constitution of Haiti and later ended its occupation.

clues.clue: Began the Bracero Program admitting Mexican farmworkers to remedy a wartime labor shortage; his successor ended the program
examples: To remedy a labor shortage, this leader hired Mexican farmworkers under the Bracero Program, which his successor ended.

clues.clue: Secretary of State Cordell Hull sent Walt Disney on a tour of South America as part of the Good Neighbor policy
examples: This man's Secretary of State, Cordell Hull, sent Walt Disney on a tour of South America as part of his Good Neighbor policy.

works.name: Path to World War II
works.description: As tensions with Japan and Germany rose, Roosevelt's presidency saw the 1937 Japanese attack on the American gunboat USS Panay, which sparked debate over the proposed Ludlow Amendment requiring a national referendum before any declaration of war. In August 1939 he received a letter -- written largely by Leo Szilard but signed by Albert Einstein -- warning that Nazi Germany could develop an atomic bomb and urging American research, which set the stage for the Manhattan Project. He supported the Quit India movement and pressed Winston Churchill to accommodate some of Mohandas Gandhi's demands while sending Britain Lend-Lease aid, and he signed the Tydings-McDuffie Act granting the Philippines a ten-year path to independence -- a colony his government later saw captured by Japan during the war, alongside the attack on Pearl Harbor.

clues.clue: Was president during the 1937 Japanese attack on the USS Panay
examples: This man was president of the United States during the Panay incident, as well as when Japan attacked Pearl Harbor.

clues.clue: The Panay incident sparked debate over the proposed Ludlow Amendment, which called for a national referendum before any declaration of war
examples: The Panay incident sparked debate over this proposed amendment, which called for a national referendum whenever Congress declared war.

clues.clue: In August 1939, received a letter written largely by Leo Szilard but signed by Albert Einstein, urging atomic bomb research before the Nazis could develop one
examples: Name this letter sent to the American president in August 1939, advising him to begin atomic bomb research before the Nazis could get their hands on the weapon.

clues.clue: Supported the Quit India movement and pressed Winston Churchill to accommodate some of Mohandas Gandhi's demands while sending Britain Lend-Lease aid
examples: This other world leader provided the main Western support for the Quit India movement and encouraged Churchill to cave to some of Gandhi's demands. As President, he sent Lend-Lease aid to Churchill.

clues.clue: Signed the Tydings-McDuffie Act granting the Philippines a ten-year path to independence; the colony was later captured by Japan under his presidency
examples: The Tydings-McDuffie Act was signed by this US president whose government saw the Japanese capture of the Philippines during World War II.

works.name: Fireside Chats
works.description: Roosevelt's roughly thirty evening radio addresses to the American public, typically opening with the address "My friends" and closing to a rendition of the "Star-Spangled Banner," were named by CBS reporter Robert Trout (sources differ on whether Harry Butcher coined the term). The first was delivered on the bank holiday he had proclaimed, explaining in simple terms how banks worked; later chats urged resistance to the Nazis under the phrase "Arsenal of Democracy." Press secretary Stephen Early helped originate the format, and speechwriter Samuel Rosenman claimed some addresses were ghostwritten by Charles Michelson and Donald Richberg; the chats directly inspired Jimmy Carter's later televised "cardigan" speech.

clues.clue: Roughly thirty evening radio addresses, typically opening with "My friends" and followed by a performance of the "Star-Spangled Banner"
examples: These works, which typically began with the address "My friends," include one urging the public to help resist the Nazis with America's "Arsenal of Democracy."
The "Star-Spangled Banner" played after each of these thirty works was delivered.

clues.clue: The first was delivered on the bank holiday he had proclaimed, explaining in simple terms how banks worked
examples: The first of them was given on a bank holiday proclaimed by their deliverer, who explained in very simple terms how banks worked.
The first of these speeches allayed the public's fear of the banking system after the "Bank Holiday" ended.

clues.clue: One chat urged Americans to help resist the Nazis by serving as the "Arsenal of Democracy"
examples: include one urging the public to help resist the Nazis with America's "Arsenal of Democracy."

clues.clue: Named by CBS reporter Robert Trout, with the format partly originated by press secretary Stephen Early

clues.clue: Speechwriter Samuel Rosenman claimed some were ghostwritten by Charles Michelson and Donald Richberg

clues.clue: Directly inspired the televised address Jimmy Carter delivered while wearing a wool cardigan
examples: CBS reporter Harry Butcher named these works, the direct inspiration for a speech that Jimmy Carter delivered while wearing a memorable wool cardigan.

works.name: The Black Cabinet and Civil Rights
works.description: Roosevelt's informal "Black Cabinet" of 45 African American advisors included Robert C. Weaver and National Youth Administration official Mary McLeod Bethune; his administration created the Fair Employment Practice Committee and, through Rayford Logan's drafted legislation, expanded the role of Black soldiers in the military. He established the Slave Narrative Collection as part of the Federal Writers' Project and sent lawyer William Hastie to the Virgin Islands before Hastie joined Howard University; his wife Eleanor Roosevelt took a heavily publicized flight with C. Alfred Anderson, the "Father of Black Aviation."

clues.clue: His informal "Black Cabinet" of 45 advisors included Robert C. Weaver and National Youth Administration official Mary McLeod Bethune
examples: Weaver and National Youth Association head Mary McLeod were among the 45 members of this president's "Black Cabinet."

clues.clue: His administration created the Fair Employment Practice Committee

examples: This politician's wife took a heavily publicized flight with the "Father of Black Aviation," C. Alfred Anderson.

works.name: Personal Life, Legacy, and Cultural Depictions
works.description: Roosevelt's personal life drew later scrutiny -- a 1994 book on his marriage recalled his continued correspondence with Lucy Mercer Rutherfurd despite his wife Eleanor's opposition, and debated whether he had an affair with his private secretary Missy LeHand; Doris Kearns Goodwin's No Ordinary Time, based on 86 interviews, similarly chronicled the Roosevelts' wartime partnership. Biographer James MacGregor Burns called him "the lion and the fox" and "the soldier of freedom," while Susan Dunn's 1940 and Robert Stinnett's conspiracy-minded Day of Deceit both examine his final years and Pearl Harbor, respectively. The composer Charles Ives sent the 63-year-old president a personal letter proposing a "People's World Union," and Roosevelt's own funeral set the precedent for playing Samuel Barber's Adagio for Strings at somber public occasions; artist Elizabeth Shoumatoff was painting his unfinished portrait at Warm Springs, Georgia on the day he died, and later replicated it from memory.

clues.clue: A 1994 book recalled his continued correspondence with Lucy Mercer Rutherfurd despite his wife's opposition, and debated whether he had an affair with his private secretary Missy LeHand
examples: A 1994 book recalls how this politician corresponded with Lucy Rutherfurd despite his wife's opposition.

examples: Doris Kearns Goodwin conducted 86 interviews for a book on this politician and his wife titled No Ordinary Time.

clues.clue: Biographer James MacGregor Burns called him "the soldier of freedom" and "the lion and the fox"
examples: This politician was called "the soldier of freedom" and "the lion and the fox" in biographies by James MacGregor Burns.

clues.clue: His final years are chronicled in Susan Dunn's book 1940

examples: Elizabeth Shoumatoff created an unfinished portrait of one member of this family before his death later that day.
Later, she replicated that portrait largely from memory in order to finish it.

comprehensive_summary: Franklin Delano Roosevelt was the 32nd U.S. president (1933-1945), who first entered federal service as Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Josephus Daniels, helping establish the North Sea Mine Barrage, before an unsuccessful 1920 vice-presidential run under James Cox and, after becoming president, rewarding Daniels with the ambassadorship to Mexico. Facing the Great Depression, Roosevelt relied on an original "Brain Trust" of Columbia professors including Rexford Tugwell and Adolf Berle, and later on Harry Hopkins, who ran the Works Progress Administration and later served as his emissary to Winston Churchill overseeing Lend-Lease, and Frances Perkins, the first female Cabinet secretary, who helped draft the 1935 Social Security Act (later upheld in Flemming v. Nestor); his administration also passed the Agricultural Adjustment Act, expanded the National Park Service through Robert Fechner's Civilian Conservation Corps, and saw its National Industrial Recovery Act "straight killing" codes struck down in the "sick chicken" case, alienating former ally Charles Coughlin. When the Supreme Court struck down New Deal measures on "Black Monday" and denied Roosevelt's authority to remove a Federal Trade Commission member in Humphrey's Executor, he responded with a court-packing plan that failed in Congress after ally Joseph Taylor Robinson's death, even as Justice Owen Roberts's "switch in time that saved nine" signaled the Court's eventual turn toward upholding his agenda on "White Monday." Breaking with two-term tradition, Roosevelt weathered Sphinx-like public speculation and a stage-managed nomination to win a third term over Wendell Willkie in 1940 (sinking the ambitions of James Farley and John Nance Garner and prompting the eventual 22nd Amendment), having already been correctly predicted to beat Alf Landon in 1936 by George Gallup's new polling company. Abroad, his Good Neighbor policy codified the declarative theory of statehood at the 1933 Montevideo Convention, sent Walt Disney touring South America under Secretary of State Cordell Hull to produce Saludos Amigos, began the Bracero Program for Mexican farmworkers, and saw his administration draft Haiti's constitution and end its U.S. occupation. As war approached, his presidency saw the 1937 Japanese attack on the USS Panay (which spurred debate over the proposed Ludlow Amendment), the August 1939 Einstein-Szilard letter warning of Nazi atomic weapons research, support for India's Quit India movement and Lend-Lease aid to Churchill, and the Tydings-McDuffie Act granting the Philippines a path to independence before Japan's wartime capture of the colony and its attack on Pearl Harbor. Throughout his presidency he addressed the nation in roughly thirty evening radio fireside chats -- opening with "My friends," partly shaped by press secretary Stephen Early and reportedly ghostwritten in part by Charles Michelson and Donald Richberg, and later the direct inspiration for Jimmy Carter's televised cardigan speech. His administration's informal "Black Cabinet," including Robert C. Weaver and Mary McLeod Bethune, pushed for the Fair Employment Practice Committee and an expanded role for Black soldiers via Rayford Logan, while Roosevelt himself established the Slave Narrative Collection and sent William Hastie to the Virgin Islands; his wife Eleanor took a publicized flight with aviator C. Alfred Anderson. His personal life drew later scrutiny over his continued correspondence with Lucy Mercer Rutherfurd and rumored relationship with secretary Missy LeHand, chronicled alongside his wartime partnership with Eleanor in Doris Kearns Goodwin's No Ordinary Time and cast by biographer James MacGregor Burns as portraying "the lion and the fox"; he died in office in April 1945 while artist Elizabeth Shoumatoff was painting his unfinished portrait at Warm Springs, Georgia, and his funeral set the precedent for playing Samuel Barber's Adagio for Strings at somber public occasions.
recursive_suggestions: Frances Perkins -- dense enough independent-answerline cluster (Triangle Shirtwaist Fire, Social Security Act, first female Cabinet secretary) to support a standalone figure page
Harry Hopkins -- recurring advisor figure with independent-answerline presence (WPA, Lend-Lease emissary); could stand alone
Charles Coughlin -- dense independent-answerline cluster ("Radio Priest," Union Party, antisemitism) on a rival political figure of the era
Eleanor Roosevelt -- recurring alongside FDR (Black aviation flight, "No Ordinary Time," Lucy Rutherfurd correspondence) with enough independent material for her own page
Einstein-Szilard letter / Manhattan Project origins -- appeared as its own answerline with dense Science-adjacent content; excluded here beyond the core fact

Ambiguity flag: "the Roosevelt family" surfaced as a joint answerline covering both FDR and Theodore Roosevelt (Sargent portrait, "Don't I!" anecdote); only the FDR-specific Shoumatoff portrait fact was retained here, and Theodore Roosevelt-specific clues were excluded

Ambiguity flag: "presidential portraits" surfaced as a distinct Fine Arts/genre answerline covering multiple presidents (Chandor, de Kooning, Chuck Close, Kehinde Wiley); excluded entirely since its actual answer is not FDR specifically

Ambiguity flag: bonus parts on Yellowstone/Delano family, Charles Ives's Essays before a Sonata, C. Wright Mills's The Power Elite, the RMS Lusitania, and Josephus Daniels/Wilmington coup surfaced FDR only as one part of a three-part bonus about a different primary topic; only the FDR-relevant sub-facts were retained
